speaker: interviewer
text: London, England, and this is tape four. You were saying about not being able to get to Israel. But you do go and visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I go and visit my daughter. And I used to went every year from 1978, '79, I'm not sure, start going as a volunteer to work in the-- in hospitals. I'm not a nurse, but they used to needed somebody to-- the hospitals, when the old people looked after who need to push them out to the open in their wheelchair and give them tea or something, chat with them because the nurses haven't got enough time. But the last time, I was four years ago. Then one of the Russian nurse told me, you-- you really done a good job, to keep coming. She speak English very well. But you see us, who is the new immigrants. They need-- we need job. And these people come as volunteers, that much less work we have. Because volunteers don't get paid, they come in-- they get food and accommodation within the hospital, but not wages. So as my daughter went to Israel as a volunteer and she settled, so every time I went to work as a volunteer, I always ended up in the kibbutz where she is. And well, she just had a baby this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the baby's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Udi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anita.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your son and your life in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my-- my son live in Birmingham. And he's always very busy. His day is sort of jobs and he's keeping him busy, like taxi driving.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he is awfully busy. He does come and visit me now and then. But he is usually quite busy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's not married, because every time he meets somebody, is-- it's not really the right person, or he's not the right one for them. So he did-- had quite a few serious friendship. But it never ended up in wedding, which is quite the shame, because I would like to see him married, and he would have a child, like my daughter. And I'm going to see the baby again shortly, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your own life here in England? How is that going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I used to be working various places, but I'm 64 now. I'm not working at all. And I used to live in Birmingham as well. We lived in Birmingham, but my-- I mean, I'm divorced now. And my son is so busy. My daughter doesn't live there. So I asked, this is the AJR, it's an Association of Jewish Refugees house, this one, belonged to the refugees. So I asked them if I could have my flat changed to come to London. Because there's a Holocaust survivor center in Hendon where I like to go. There is all sorts of groups. I belong to everything, because there's a group for child survivors, a group for camp survivors, a group for just Holocaust survivors, who-- these people survived in a variety ways. So I belong to all the groups, really, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how often are you at-- do you go to the survivor center?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go very often, because it's very interesting talks going on. And I like to-- I like to be there and entertainment as well. Not very often talk about what happened, because if we want to talk about it, we always talk as sort of one to one who happen to be sitting to a table, because usually end up with a tea and biscuit. So we usually talk about each other's experience. And most people end up crying, so we don't-- you know, it's hard. Especially the child survivors, I find them more shocked about what happened to them than the older age group. I don't know why, but the younger survivors find it very hard. And they wasn't grown up yet. And they all came to the conclusion, even the child survivors like me, I think I'm the youngest who survived Auschwitz really. I'm 64 now, and I still fear sometime when I see all the women who would be roughly the age as my mother, I feel [SOBBING] sometime my mother could have been lived up till now. I mean, seen my children, you know. It's very hurtful. She was only 34, 35. I mean it's a very young age to-- to lose. Because if somebody died from illness or old age, people can accept that this is the way life goes. But to destroy a young life, it's really terrible. My father was only 44, 43, actually, when he start going to this forced labor. I mean, for a man as well is a young age.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back over what, what you went through do you have any-- any explanations for yourself as to why you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wouldn't have survived if I wouldn't go on extreme, because quite honestly after later when I-- many times I find-- many questions I'll find later. If my mother wasn't selected to-- to that other group, and I wasn't escaping from that crowd of people who I was in when-- you know, the other side of this washroom. And I went-- they recognize now I'm running. And because one shoe had stepped out of in the mud, they recognized, they guessed I was there and was looking for me. But I was-- I was just hiding in that. I mean for a-- for a 13 years old is a terrible-- is a terrible experience to go through. But if my mother wasn't selected to go to this other group who was going to work, I wouldn't be in around, because that group who I was selected to, they was going to the gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was wondering with the fact that your life in Hungary to begin with was not religious, you almost didn't know what, what Judaism was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what it must have then felt like to have been-- gone through what you did go through for something that you kind of knew, because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's what I'm afraid of schools the rest of my life. I don't wear anything which is recognized and I don't go on the street wearing anything which make people recognize that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the time, until recently I was working. And I never said no, I'm Jewish to people who never asked for it. And even if they asked, I used to say, oh, I have no religion. I-- I mean everybody have a religion. But they use it and go to church or go to synagogue, but everybody have a religion, some kind, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I used to say that I have no religion, and I think this is the truth. Because I don't go to-- I mean, quite honestly, I don't go-- I belong to the synagogue nearby, because I paid for, if-- whenever I die, to be buried. But I never go. Because I've made it quite clear to these people who is doing that administration, is everybody getting maybe differently affected by what they went through. I'm affected by it like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, if I go to a, a proper synagogue, because nowadays it's reform synagogue, everybody on the ground floor and men and women sit next to one another. But if I go to a synagogue, which is the old-fashioned synagogue, where women sit on the gallery and the men downstairs, and wearing this tallis, it remind me of the picture when I was a child. And all those crowds of men and women who prayed and kept their kitchen up to the Jewish religious demand, what happened to them? I just-- i, I just can't. I-- I'm afraid I can't understand how anybody, if they've gone through what I've been through, how they can go to synagogues and not to have the same picture front of them when they was young what happened to all these people. Actually, my grandmother-- divorced from my grandfather. But they both was quite religious. And my grandfather expected me to say, you know, like-- like in Christian religion to say something before meal, you know, thanks for having meal and all that. I mean he was really religious, and what happened to him? I don't know what happened to him, because I haven't seen him since-- I think it was 1943. I haven't seen him. You see, I just-- I can't see what happened to these people, and they all kept to the religious laws, so what, what was the point? Who was listening to all those prayers? I just can't-- I can't get over it. But it sounds like, while you're not a practicing Jew, going to the survivor center and being with other Jewish people is very important to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, well I'm jewish. Yes, I'm Jewish, and I-- I belong to this, well, this sort of Jews who survived. Because if I'm among those Jews whose parents, grandparents, and great-grandparents, everybody lived in peace, they are not really understand my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] way of my, my thinking. And I don't think we could see eye to eye, especially since some people are extremely religious. No, I, I, I don't think it's, it's possible. You know, I sent my daughter, not my son, because where we lived when my son was young, but it wasn't a Jewish school. But where we was living in Birmingham, quite near to a Jewish school called King David School. I sent my daughter to that school, because I thought, let her learn what I can't teach her, because I can't remember. Even, even, even if-- whatever was kept, I can't remember. Because you know, young kids wasn't hanging about in kitchen, especially before the war. That was cooker, which had to, in our place, had to put fire burning. And it was dangerous to be messing about, for a child in a kitchen like this. So I always be sent out, so I wouldn't know how to, you know.

words.text: Yes, yes, well I'm jewish. Yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any kind of message that you would leave, following your experience for your-- for Udi and for any other grandchildren you may have in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SOBBING] I went through all this, and he must know full of my, my thinking, because it's a different age. He must, if he like to be keeping the religion, he have to. But don't take any-- I said to my children, don't take any future for their own thinking because of me. Because it's what happened to me, it-- it made me a different person than if it never would have happened. So when the life is no more, then if they want to keep religion, they can keep religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what your life-- what aspirations, do you remember what aspirations you had for your life as a young-- as a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my-- yes, my parents used to thought I was so good at drawing, when, when I was a young child. I mean wasn't think of within the Jewish community or anything, but they thought I-- when I get older, finish school, they were going to send me to some, some kind of school where I can learn drawing properly. And I could draw faces. I could draw, you know-- like, I used to draw the corner of the kitchen or the room or out in the garden was drawing in this-- it was-- it was a courtyard, but there's plenty of plants and hedge and-- I just to been drawing that as well. So they thought when I get a bit, when I finish a certain much schooling, then they send me to learn how to paint or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that something you thought of doing after the-- after the war at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had to earn my keep. And I was-- I was-- I wasn't always well. I had to keep going for a-- for a treatment or another. And this job was fine, because they let me go. I mean it's such a huge number of people worked in that factory, I mean. If I was out for a few hours, it wasn't really matter. And I could earn as much to support myself from-- really from the age of say 15, 16. I wasn't-- called learner, but I was [INAUDIBLE] still the same thing. But I was start earning enough to keep myself, very poorly, because you know [INAUDIBLE] everything piecework, had to work like machine, you know, piecework to be able to earn. But I could keep myself. So it was never occurred to me to study as well as work. It was just six days a week work, you know. And shift work, actually. Is turn, you know, day time, evening, and night shift, eight hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you do-- do you go to art classes at the survivor center?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't, because I-- since I'm back in London-- because I used to live in London when I came to England, and I was in Birmingham. Since I'm back in London, I had everything going wrong with my health. I had to go for an operation on my tummy. I had to go for one eye and then the other eye. I'm only back two years ago. And I went-- kept going to Israel to visit my daughter. So I do go on Sundays, definitely. And occasionally during the week if there's something interesting going on. But what I, I really would like to go to regularly when I come back-- I'm going to see my my little grandson. And when I come back, I'm really serious, and [INAUDIBLE] start learn Hebrew classes. Because when I'm there, I understand quite a bit. But I can't say very much. So I find it interesting to learn. Actually, is I thought it was awfully difficult, but once I went to an evening class but it's gone from me. But I don't think it's all that difficult. I mean my daughter speak absolutely perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's your challenge for the future,

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. In the autumn, they will start the evening-- not evening, daytime class in the center I'm going to, yeah. And then I meet various nationality people, because I only know what happened to me. And I really want to hear what happened to other people. So I'm going to various. Also we had a, a plan to do that public speaking. And I went for this, and I just enough to cope, not to cry. Because I really dearly would like to speak to people to say what happened to me, not all, from start to end, but little bit. But I'm so difficult to keep my tears. I got to learn how to, because they do, do explain how to keep strong. I was going a couple of times for this public speaking course, and I seen women and men who was talking for a little while, even among us, and then [INAUDIBLE] I see their hands shaking, holding the tissues like-- and he wasn't crying. But it was like that. And then the talk couldn't go any further. So these people who is really experienced now-- so I suppose it will take me time. I really would like to learn how to be strong and be able to say. Because it really hurt me when, when I hear these terrible remarks, it never happened. So it have to be said by people like me, went through all this. This is the most terrible thing to happen to me to hear this never happened. But it is the most terrible [INAUDIBLE] been said when I was in Israel. And he says, you better come. You can live in Israel, because anybody who is Jewish or half-Jewish is free to be in Israel. But we can't support you. You have to be 100% on private health care and have to support yourself and I can't. That's another very painful thing to-- for my background.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, is there anything else that you'd like to add, anything we've missed, or any other thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because it isn't possible to-- it's not important to go into details what happened. Because, you know, during this course of this time, in Auschwitz and in, in Camp Mauthausen, because it was just going the same, all every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing else you want to add at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Parker, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was taken 1929, when my parents got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? [SOBBING] Janos Hochhauser and Magda Goldfinger. Magda Goldfinger. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] --was in the-- in the holiday we went, and that was in a-- in a country place. And see this well behind me and the chickens? I was only little. Don't know exactly how old I was. This is you in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yes. This is my grandmother on the left, I'm on-- in the middle, and my mother on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margaret.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your mother's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken? In Pestszenterzsebet. I was, I think, four in that. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Budapest zoo. We had a day out when-- I don't know. I think I was 7, 8 I'm not sure. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] That's my parents and myself. I don't know, 7, 8 years old in, in our garden in the summer. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yes, this is the last picture. My, my father in the background and my father's brother came from the forced labor, on and off they came out. And that was the last time. My, my auntie in the background, and my mother, my father's side grandparents and me. That was the last time any of us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your father's parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, the Hochhausers, Aron, the grandfather and Julia, grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our garden in Pestszenterzsebet. outside of our windows, outside of our-- is a-- is a big courtyard, it was. Yes, that was the last picture I have from school. This is a school uniform actually I have on. I was 11. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Red Cross document, which by chance somebody, a social worker be able to find it for me in 1977. But this is the date when I was in Auschwitz and when I went to Camp Mauthausen. And that was my maiden name, Klara Hochhauser. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So fine, but is it readable? Hm? It's very fine. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of those what they give me in Israel as I used to go to volunteer work. I got seven of them. And this is one. I was working in Israel as a volunteer. This is my son and daughter. My son live in Birmingham. My daughter live in Israel in a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex, my son, and Anita, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This last summer they was here, Anita's with her husband. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is Udi, my grandson, who was born 19-- 1991. Oh, 1996! Oh, my goodness. He born 1996, 29th of March, Udi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my daughter Anita on her wedding, and my son-in-law, who is-- who is [INAUDIBLE] Gabay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
